speaker: interviewer
text: Wait. I'm not zoning. [PAUSES FOR 10 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, you were telling us about your sister and her friend.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. There was Hirszke Kliksman, who was coming over on Sundays to visit my sister. And he would bring food for us. I had never known any Polish Jews, any Kovonoa Jews. And there was always this-- this feeling I understand today, and I knew, as I learned, as time went on, that there was not a very good relationship between Polish Jews and-- and German Jews-- for whatever reason it was. It's-- this is not the time to go into this. In any event, my mother became very fond of Hirszke, and so was my sister. And he invited me over to the Latvian ghetto to come to spend time with him and his friend, whose name was Monya Dobren. And this is-- this an experience-- I have to smile. I'm smiling inside-- which I never forget-- and I've talked about it many a times. The food we had eaten till that time was very poor. It was very poor diet. We were getting bread and potatoes, but very little anything else. And I went over that Sunday afternoon to Hirszke's and Monya's apartment, a house where they lived. And they had a crude stove in their room.

speaker: interviewer
text: And Hirszke asked me, Ludwig, you want to eat something? I said, yes, I am hungry. I'd love to eat something. And he made a pot of potatoes with smothered onions, in fat, with-- I don't know. You know what [NON-ENGLISH] are?

speaker: interviewee
text: The leftovers when-- when you-- when you render fat-- really rich food. And my stomach hadn't had rich food for a year, I suppose. And he made those mashed potatoes with the onions and the [NON-ENGLISH]. And that, to me, tasted like the best thing I've ever eaten in my life. And I ate, and I ate, and I ate. And needless to say, my system couldn't absorb it. And I became very sick afterwards because of the richness of the food. Nevertheless, I fondly look back at that memory, because that meant so well, [LAUGHS] and I got sick. But I became very friendly with-- with both of the young men. They were about my age. As a matter of fact, Harry is-- Hirszke is my age. And Monya was a few years younger than-- than I was. The relationship between Hirszke and-- and my sister stayed, it kept-- kept on growing, and it was-- it was a-- a good thing for us, for the family, knowing that there was some love and affection still around. And their whole relationship was just-- just great. Just to retrace myself a little bit, there was a school, an informal school, which was available for Jewish children, which was in a family's apartment where they lived. Jewish children used to congregate there. And a-- I cannot recall his name anymore. The gentleman was a teacher in Germany. He used to teach the basics of the three R's-- writing, reading, and arithmetic. Two children and, I believe, my sister, went to this particular classroom when she first got into-- into the Riga ghetto. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Shortly after, my-- my father was still in Salaspils. And shortly after, I had gone to work at this automobile repair shop. We went to work one morning. And at work, they told us that we are not going back to the ghetto, that they were keeping us in town. There was a house. They were going to put us up-- that it was too much work, too many autos, to repair. And they wanted to save time to get us to work in the morning. Of course, I knew what-- my-- my mother would be absolutely besides herself not knowing that-- where I was, and that I wasn't coming back that night. Because that time already, shootings and hangings, and all kinds of killings were going on. For whatever reason, they could find to kill someone. For example, people who used to work out in the ghetto-- out in the city-- would come home with some bread, which they would have been given by some decent people outside the city. They were searched. They'd bring the back-- they were searched. Bread would be found on them. They were arrested on the spot. They would-- the Nazis would bring everybody together in a [NON-ENGLISH] plots. I can't think of the word in English right now-- a place where-- an assembly-- into assembly. They had gallows standing there, which were part of the scenery. And they would announce, 'This man brought bread in from-- from the ghetto,' rather, from the city into the ghetto. He was caught, and put a noose around his neck. And they would hang him. And there were times when there were four or five people hanging on these gallows, and they would leave those people hanging there for two or three days to remind everybody to behave, or else. And not coming back from this work commando, I knew my-- my mother would be very much disturbed in not knowing what was going on. We knew my father was in Salaspils. But of course, they didn't know where I was. That night, I sneaked out of this work compound. And we were still wearing civilian clothes, civic clothes. And I walked through the city by myself, snuck into the ghetto, which was not too difficult, provided you didn't get caught; told my mother where I was-- told my family where I was; snuck out of the ghetto again, walked through the city, and back to the compound where we were staying, and-- just for them to have peace of mind. The compound itself was not too bad. We worked there 12, 14, hours a day repairing cars, fixing automobiles. There were some people that really were knowledgeable about it-- Jews who had been recruited, who had prior experience on cars. Rations were poor. But the shadows of the gallows and the guns going off every now and then in the ghetto, were not hanging all over our heads. Consequently, the lot I was given was better than having stayed in the ghetto itself. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I stayed at this work station for-- for quite a while. It dissolved. I went back in the ghetto again. And by that time, working conditions-- the conditions of my mother, and my two aunts, and my sister-- physical conditions-- they really were-- were very bad. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] My mother became very ill. And-- [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] --she died. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] She died in the ghetto. And part of the ghetto-- and I suppose there was a reason for them putting the ghetto where it was-- was the old Jewish cemetery of Riga. And we were able to bury my mother in Riga Jewish-- Jewish Cemetery-- [DRINKS] --which was in [NON-ENGLISH] in 1943-- [NON-ENGLISH]. I'll never forget that. My aunts and my sister were still living there, and I. And my father is still in Salaspils. Things were really, really bad. And by that time, a lot of people were not able to perform work anymore. And the Nazi, the Gestapo head, organized transports where they would segregate and separate people-- well people from poor-- from-- from sick people-- and put them in a truck. And they were gonna resettle them again-- quote, resettle. But of course, we knew, by that time what resettling meant. It didn't mean to go out and work on a farm, to be productive workers, and live like decent human beings. My aunts were picked up. They were taken, put in a truck, and taken away. That must have been the middle part of 1943. And the worst part of it all was not that they were just taken away. There was a [NON-ENGLISH], a sorting station for clothes in the ghetto where, once they would take the people to Rumbula, which was the place where they would kill 'em-- shoot 'em, they would undress them, or have to undress themselves. And they would bring the clothes back. And the clothes, then, were sorted in this sorting station in the ghetto. And of course, the sorting was done by our own people, by Jews who were designated to do that type of work. And word would come out when these people would come from work. Well, we found the clothes of so-and-so. Or we found a note in the clothes of so-and-so. And that particular day, when my aunts were taken, my Aunt Flora had a note in her pocket-- who she was; somehow, some kinda-- kind of identity. And that night, someone came to me and told me that the clothes off Aunt Flora had come back on that truck where she had been taken out to the [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: Needless to say, we know by then what was happening-- took people out, and had them undress themselves; took them out to mass graves and shot 'em. Riga didn't have extermination or crematorias. It was still in the beginning stages of the era. Needless to say, you started pondering in your mind when is it your turn? When are you gonna go?

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It's a terrible, terrible time for everybody. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It was-- it was a time where a lot of people contemplate suicide and committed suicide. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] There really was no hope anymore, at that particular time, which was 1943, middle of 1943. I was still in-- in the ghetto when I was working, in and out every day. And I was able, being out there, always to get a hold of some extra food-- piece of bread, one potato, whatever it might have been, just to carry it in and supplement whatever little we were getting-- to keep us alive. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] In the meantime, obviously, other groups-- I forgot to mention that other groups from Germany had arrived in the ghetto. There was a group from Cologne, a group from Westphalia, a group from Vienna. And the populous of the ghetto really had grown. But it really didn't change anything-- just that there were more of us-- more problems, an untenable situation, despair.

speaker: interviewer
text: And yet, with all of the despair, with all of the horror, there was some good times, in spite of it all. I remember there was a group of young boys, inclusive, myself. We used to play soccer on Sunday. Thinking of it today, I said, how did we do it, you know? How did we get the mental and physical strength to play soccer? We used to see girls. How did we do it? We did, in spite of everything what was going on. And I believe that was part of what kept myself, or me, keep my sanity-- that there were other things besides horror. And I was able to separate one from the other.

speaker: interviewee
text: In 19-- the end of 1943, the process of closing the ghetto in Riga started. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: There was an outside-- [DRINKS] --the-- the process was started in the middle or end of 1943, just to-- to liquidate the ghetto. Liquidate meaning what?

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Those of us who were capable and able to work were sent to Kaiserwald. Kaiserwald was a concentration camp. It was not a ghetto anymore where we lived with our families together. It was a barbed wire enclave with barracks, with wooden cots, where five or six people slept on a cot, where men and women were separated in separate camps. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And of course, we hoped that things would be better-- would become better. We had no idea what was in the future. But you always hoped for the best. My father, at the time, was still outside, working outside in a [NON-ENGLISH]. My sister, by that time, was also working outside the ghetto for the German army, sorting clothes-- not going back to the ghetto anymore. And I was the only one left in the ghetto who was, then, liquidated with the rest of the people and put into Kaiserwald, which was this-- this camp unbeknownst to any of us. Situations in Kaiserwald were much different than in-- in ghetto-- much more rigid, much more organized-- as far as the Germans were concerned-- the SS was concerned. There was no more Jewish leadership role played by any Jew. We were all ruled by the SS and a group of German criminals, who were criminals, in a sense, that they were either convicted of murder within Germany, political crimes of-- any type of a civil disobedient law-- if that's a term-- I don't know-- violating the laws of the-- of the-- of the country, of Germany. And these people were put in charge of the inside of the camp. [PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS] By orders of the-- of the SS leadership, these people who were criminals were in charge of us, the Jews. And the situation became from bad to worse, because what the SS couldn't do and didn't do to-- to hurt us, these criminals would. They were very, very powerful. Just to-- I can think of three individuals. One was-- X. His name was X. I learned that he was a murderer-- had murdered five or six people-- and he was in charge of the men's camp. He had a few surrogates who also were criminals. One was Hannes. And there were also several Polish criminals who had been convicted of crimes in Germany, who also were in charge of-- of the camps-- of the Jewish-- of the men. And these-- these people were just merciless. I would say they were worse than the SS was. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] And again, we used to go to work on the outside of-- of Kaiserwald, where we performed work, but under much worse conditions than it was out of the ghetto. Food rations were cut in 1/2 from what we had in ghetto. Working conditions were worse than we had in ghetto. Living conditions were 10 times worse than we had in the ghetto. In ghetto, if somebody got sick, there were still some way and some place to help people get better. In Kaiserwald, there was a sanitarium. Once you went into the sanitarium, which was a-- supposed to be a clinic, which was run by an SS doctor and a few criminals, you usually never come out of it-- alive. So regardless how sick you were-- and once we found out what was going on in this medical building, you-- you wouldn't dare go in. It was, approximately, the end of '43 that I got a break in life. Again, we were standing out one morning on a 5-- 5 o'clock, 4:30, 5 o'clock we were put out, standing to-- to be counted. The man who was running the camp was SS-- SS Sturmbannfuhrer Zauer. After we were all counted and-- and accounted for, he came out and ask if anyone was capable of repairing automobiles, and someone capable of welding. And I was amongst many people who raised their hand that I was able of-- of doing that kind of work. And he picked me. Of all the men and young boys who were there who had raised their hand, he picked me. He says, I want you to come with me. And I went with him. And he took me outside the-- outside the-- the lager concentration camp-- just about, maybe, 50-- 50 feet outside the gate. And that was his office and his garage. And he had two cars. And he asked me if I was capable of keeping his cars in order. I said, yes, I was. And he said, well, you have a job. I want you-- I want you to keep my cars in tip top shape. I want them washed and cleaned every day. If there's any repair to be done, I'm gonna tell you what needs to be done. I want you to do it. And I will reward you for it. I said, yes, I'll be happy to do that kind of work. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I was all by myself, working by myself, doing the job he asked me to do. And the second day I was working there, when he came in from his home in the morning, he knew my name by then. He said, Strauss, there's something in my car for you. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I looked in his car. In his backseat, there were a couple of sandwiches in his car. And again, I thought, this guy is trying to play games with me. Food is probably poisoned. And I'm gonna be very careful.

speaker: interviewer
text: But then, I rationalized. I said, this man doesn't have to poison me, because I had seen him walking through camp with his pistol in his hand and shooting people in the head. So if he wants to get rid of me, why does he have to poison me?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ate the sandwiches-- best I had eaten in-- in four years, three years. It's incredibly-- I mean, it was bread-- bread, with butter, and meat and sausage. And I couldn't believe what was happening to me. It was like-- like-- like-- like a miracle. And he kept feeding me. Everyday he'd come. He'd say, I got something in my car for you. The guy [LAUGHS] liked me. For what reason, I don't know. And he got two more cars. And I told him that I couldn't do the work by myself anymore. I needed some help.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he said, OK, fine. Get yourself some help. Who do you want? And as Hirszke Kliksman, who had treated me to that mashed potatoes with onions, who was a friend of my sister, was also in Kaiserwald.

speaker: interviewee
text: I told the commandant that I wanted him to help me, that he was a friend of mine. And without question, he sent one of the SS guards out, picked up Hirszke, brought him out into the garage. And from that point on, Hirszke was working with me. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Fortunately, I had enough food there to feed Hirszke. And by that time, I had really become well acquainted with the commandant-- with Zauer-- acquainted enough that I was able to talk to him things of something other than just the cars.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I said to him one day, I said, hey, commandant, is it possible that I could-- that you could bring my father here? My father is in a work commando outside of Riga. And I would sure like to be together with him.

speaker: interviewee
text: He asked me where my father was.
